text: The essential point of port placement in the robotic lobectomy is to place the camera and instruments in correct relation to the fissure and the target anatomy.
This is one technique for accomplishing this.
First, the tip of the scapula is identified and marked.
Secondly, the location of the fissure is estimated by placing the hand one hand's width below the scapular mark and marking the inner space.
Then a mark is placed in the anatomic center of the chest directly below this.
This is the estimated camera site.
Be sure this is in the anatomic center to optimize the use of gravity during the course of the procedure.
This is for instrument arm number two.
This is the same marking viewed from the posterior aspect.
The position for instrument arm three is one hand's breadth, or nine centimeters, superior and inferior to that of instrument arm two in a relatively tissue-free position.
Note that it is important to achieve adequate AP distance between these two sites, as if this is not accomplished, external arm collisions will occur.
With this scheme, camera and instrument arm two are in the same inner space,
instrument arm 3 can be placed into the inner space if that is desired.
The site for instrument arm 1 is identified by placing the hand one hand's breadth or 9 centimeters anteriorly and superior to the camera and marking a spot.
It is important to maintain a less than 45 degree angle between this instrument and the hyler plane in the event that stapling has to be done through this port.
The position of instrument arm 1 is the most forgiving and moving it down in interspace rarely causes issue unless it compromises the accessory port.
The position of the accessory port is at the apex of a triangle equidistant between instrument arm 1 and the camera at the costal margin.
It is important to be equidistant here because your assistant has to work under these arms.
Incisions made along the costal margin and a tunnel created to the intercostal space above it.
This will be for the initial port placement.
If transdiaphragmatic resection is required, this incision would be extended and a small subcutaneous tunnel created inferiorly to retrieve the specimen.
Port size selection is an important consideration.
Instrument arm 3 is a 5 or 8 millimeter port depending on selected instruments.
Instrument 2 is an 8 millimeter port.
Camera is a 12 millimeter port.
Note the relationship of the camera port to the posterior axillary line.
Once port site selection has been made, the patient is prepped and draped.
In this scheme, a varis needle is introduced through instrument arm 1 and the chest insufflated to a pressure of 10 in order to recreate the operative field in which one will be working.
Once a pressure of 10 is achieved, an incision is made in the skin and the rib overlying the site of the camera port selection.
For lower lobes, the interspace below this is then chosen.
For upper lobes, that above it is chosen.
The port is introduced through the selected interspace.
The robotic camera is then introduced through the port, visualizing the operative field.
This port has the advantage of sandwiching the chest wall between the inflated balloon and the compressed foam cuff.
It is manufactured by Covidien and has the advantage of minimizing inadvertent camera extraction.
CO2 insufflation is removed from the varis needle and placed on the camera and it is a good habit to get into to clean the camera board every time the camera is removed to minimize smudging.
Introduction of the camera demonstrates the correct relationship between the camera and the fissure.
The varus needle has been left in place, demonstrating the location of the proposed site for instrument arm one.
Note its relation to the anterior aspect of the oblique fissure, ideally situated in the event it is required for stapling and excellent for dissection and retraction.
The process of port placement has then begun.
The incision is made in the skin, slightly smaller than the opening of the eight millimeter port.
We use a hemostat to dilate the hole and then the port is introduced.
The port is placed initially in a position deeper into the chest than that in which it will finally rest.
This is because it is very common for the port to be backed out during the course of docking.
The identical sequence of events is carried out for instrument arm 2.
Note the digital pressure on the interspace demonstrates the proposed site.
In this circumstance, instrument 2 is ideally placed just slightly below the apex of the posterior aspect of the oblique fissure.
In this case, adhesions between the upper lobe and the chest wall nearly obscured the selected site for instrument arm number three.
The port site incision and puncture wound were made for illustrative purposes, but the port and instruments were not introduced until the lung had been safely dissected off of the chest wall.
Note its relation to the posterior aspect of the oblique fissure, centrally at the apex.

This allows an optimal range of motion with retraction of the upper lobe as well as accessing the posterior aspects and inferior aspects of the chest cavity for division of the inferior pulmonary ligament.

Although it appears that this incision in portside selection is extremely posterior, once the lung had been dissected free from the chest wall, it was evident that this was indeed in the appropriate position, posterior laterally.
Finally, the accessory port is introduced.
The camera is turned around manually.
Incision made at the cost of margin and the interspace identified through which the 15 millimeter trocar will be introduced.
The diaphragm at this site is also inspected to ensure that there are no contraindications to transdiaphragmatic removal of the specimen in case that is required.
With placement of this port, port placement is complete.
Once port placement is complete, the robot is brought in over the patient's head with the center column in direct line with the accessory port.
This optimizes room for arms two and three.
A final point regarding arm position.
Difficulty reaching the posterior or inferior aspects of the thoracic cavity can be accomplished by temporarily rotating the arm inward, as pictured here.
This increases the range of the arm and allows access to the lower portions of the thoracic cavity.
